title: Banoffee Pie
subtitle: Digestive biscuit base, toffee made from condensed milk, bananas, and whipped cream
excerpt: A press-in biscuit base topped with a thick, deeply caramelised toffee made by boiling condensed milk, sliced bananas, and softly whipped double cream. No baking required. The toffee and the bananas set in the fridge; the cream goes on just before serving.
sourceNotes: Banoffee pie was created by Nigel Mackenzie and Ian Dowding at The Hungry Monk restaurant in Jevington, East Sussex, in 1972. The original used condensed milk boiled in the tin to make toffee, combined with bananas and cream on a pastry base. The biscuit base version became the domestic standard and the pie is now one of the most widely made British desserts.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the base and toffee and add the bananas up to 24 hours ahead. Add the cream just before serving.

content.text: The toffee is the core of this pie. It is made by cooking condensed milk with butter and brown sugar until it thickens and darkens to a proper toffee consistency, not the colour of the condensed milk it started as, but a deep amber. Undercooked toffee stays pale and sweet; properly cooked toffee is darker, slightly bitter, and much more interesting. Stir constantly and watch the colour change.

The bananas go on top of the cold toffee. Very ripe bananas are better than underripe ones, they have more flavour and the texture is softer. Add the whipped cream just before serving; if it goes on too early it will collapse under its own weight.
A 20 cm springform tin. The springform clip makes removing and slicing the pie much easier than a fixed-base tin.

Crush {{digestive-biscuit}} of digestive biscuits to fine crumbs in a food bag with a rolling pin, or blitz in a food processor. Mix with {{unsalted-butter}} of melted butter until the crumbs are evenly coated. Press firmly into the base of a 20 cm springform tin, spreading to an even layer about 5 mm deep. Refrigerate for 30 minutes to set.

Melt 50 g of butter and {{light-brown-sugar}} of light brown sugar together in a medium saucepan over medium heat, stirring until the sugar dissolves. Add {{condensed-milk}} of condensed milk and bring to a gentle boil, stirring constantly. Boil for 5, 7 minutes, stirring continuously, until the toffee darkens to a deep amber and thickens enough to coat the back of the spoon. It will bubble vigorously, be careful. Pour immediately over the chilled biscuit base and spread evenly. Refrigerate for at least 1 hour until set.

Slice {{banana}} of bananas and arrange over the set toffee. Whip {{double-cream}} of double cream to soft peaks and spoon or pipe over the bananas. Serve immediately, or keep refrigerated for up to 2 hours before the cream begins to soften.

items.symptom: Toffee is pale and very sweet, not caramelised
items.cause: Not cooked long enough, or heat was too low
items.fix: Cook for the full 7 minutes at a steady boil, stirring constantly. The toffee should visibly darken from a pale gold to a deep amber during cooking.

items.cause: Heat was too high or left unattended
items.fix: Stir continuously and keep the heat at medium, a gentle boil, not a rolling boil. If it starts to smell bitter, remove from the heat immediately.

items.symptom: Biscuit base is crumbly and doesn't hold together when cut
items.cause: Not enough butter, or base was not pressed firmly
items.fix: Press firmly with the back of a spoon until very compact. Add an extra 10 g of melted butter if the crumbs seem dry.
Add bananas no more than 2 hours before serving. A squeeze of lemon juice tossed through the sliced bananas delays browning slightly.

content.text: Banoffee pie was created in 1972 at The Hungry Monk restaurant in Jevington, East Sussex, by Nigel Mackenzie and Ian Dowding. The original used the then-new technique of boiling an unopened tin of condensed milk to produce a caramel, combined with bananas and cream on a pastry base. The name combines 'banana' and 'toffee'. The recipe spread through British home cooking during the 1970s and 1980s and the biscuit-base version, easier to make than the pastry original, became the domestic standard. It is now one of the few British desserts with a documented invention rather than a gradual development.
